sets.title: Preterite vs Imperfect
sets.description: Both are past tenses, but the choice encodes how the speaker frames the action: as a bounded event (preterite) or as ongoing, habitual, or background state (imperfect).

pairs.sentence_b: De niño, comía manzanas todos los días.
pairs.question: Which sentence describes a single finished action?
pairs.explanation: "Comí" (preterite) presents the eating as a discrete, completed event at a specific past moment. "Comía" (imperfect) presents it as a recurring habit — not a single event but an ongoing pattern. Same verb, different viewpoints on time.

pairs.sentence_a: Cuando entré, María leía un libro.
pairs.sentence_b: Cuando entré, María leyó un libro.
pairs.question: Which sentence implies María was already in the middle of reading when you arrived?
pairs.explanation: "Leía" (imperfect) sets a background scene — the reading was already in progress. "Leyó" (preterite) presents a second completed event, as if entering and reading were sequential steps. The imperfect is the camera that pans across the scene; the preterite is the plot point.

pairs.sentence_a: El verano pasado, visitamos tres países.
pairs.sentence_b: Cada verano, visitábamos a los abuelos.
pairs.question: Which sentence describes something that happened repeatedly over many years?
pairs.explanation: "Visitamos" (preterite) counts three countries as a completed tally. "Visitábamos" (imperfect) describes an annual ritual — no single occasion, but a pattern that stretched across years.

sets.title: Ser vs Estar
sets.description: Both translate as "to be", but ser expresses inherent identity or permanent categories, while estar expresses condition, state, or location — things that can change.

pairs.question: Which sentence says the coffee has been left to cool down (not normally cold)?
pairs.explanation: "Es frío" (ser) declares cold as an inherent, defining quality — like a cold brew. "Está frío" (estar) reports a current condition: the coffee was once hot and has since cooled. The estar sentence implies change; the ser sentence implies nature.

pairs.question: Which sentence means the person is experiencing boredom right now?
pairs.explanation: "Es aburrido" (ser) characterizes the person — they are boring by nature. "Está aburrido" (estar) reports an emotional state — they are bored at this moment. The same adjective carries opposite meanings depending on the copula.

sets.title: Subjunctive vs Indicative
sets.description: The indicative reports facts; the subjunctive encodes doubt, desire, emotion, or unreality. The choice changes what the speaker claims about the world.

pairs.sentence_a: Busco un apartamento que tenga balcón.
pairs.sentence_b: Busco el apartamento que tiene balcón.
pairs.question: Which speaker has a specific apartment in mind?
pairs.explanation: "Tenga" (subjunctive) expresses that the apartment is not yet identified — it may not even exist. "Tiene" (indicative) signals the speaker knows which apartment. One word shifts the meaning from searching for any candidate to referring to a specific one.

pairs.question: Which sentence expresses a personal hope rather than a belief?
pairs.explanation: "Espero que venga" (subjunctive) encodes desire — the speaker wants the person to come but cannot assert it. "Creo que viene" (indicative) encodes a belief — the speaker thinks it will happen. Hope vs. prediction.

sets.title: Por vs Para
sets.description: Both translate loosely as "for", but por looks backward (cause, exchange, duration, agent) while para looks forward (purpose, recipient, destination, deadline).

pairs.label_a: because of you / on your behalf
pairs.question: Which sentence means you were the reason or motivation for the action?
pairs.explanation: "Por ti" (por) means because of you — you were the cause or the pressure that motivated it. "Para ti" (para) means intended for you — you are the recipient or beneficiary. Same words, opposite directions of time and agency.

pairs.label_b: studying in preparation for the exam
pairs.question: Which sentence implies the exam is an upcoming goal?
pairs.explanation: "Para el examen" (para) frames the exam as a future target — all effort is aimed at it. "Por el examen" (por) suggests the exam is a cause of stress that drives the studying. Goal-directed vs. cause-driven.
